chunk_summary: This section outlines protocols for cold chain transport and chain-of-custody documentation of expressed breast milk (EBM) and donor human milk (DHM), emphasizing temperature control, documentation accuracy, and incident reporting for safe handling and use.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of maintaining an unbroken cold chain for EBM and DHM transport.
Learn the documentation requirements for chain-of-custody during milk transport and dispensing.
Identify protocols for handling partially thawed or fully thawed milk upon receipt.
Explore strategies for incident reporting and quality assurance in milk transport systems.
key_facts: EBM and DHM must be transported at ≤4°C (refrigerated) or frozen using insulated containers and frozen gel packs.
Chain-of-custody documentation includes container identifiers, volumes, dates, dispatch/receiving times, and staff signatures.
Partially thawed milk at ≤4°C should be refrigerated and used within 24 hours; fully thawed or warm milk must be discarded unless local policies allow safe use.
Cold-chain failures require incident reporting and review of packaging, courier timing, and backup power plans.
Temperature indicators or data loggers are recommended for high-volume milk networks to ensure transport temperature compliance.
reference_quote: Transport of EBM or DHM must maintain an unbroken cold chain from the point of expression or dispensing to the point of use.
document_key_topics: Cold chain transport protocols for EBM and DHM
Handling thawed milk and incident reporting
Quality assurance in milk transport systems
